title: Reading and Use of English - Part 5
description: You are going to read an article about reading and books. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: The Space Inside a Book
content.subtitle: What reading does to us — and why it still matters
content.text:
Reading is one of the strangest activities that human beings have invented. We sit still, our eyes moving across a flat surface, and find ourselves elsewhere — inside other minds, other lives, other centuries. [31]Unlike film or theatre, which present images and sound to a relatively passive viewer, reading requires the active participation of the reader's imagination in the construction of the world the text describes. Every reader creates a subtly different version of the book they read.[/31]

The cognitive neuroscientist Maryanne Wolf has described reading as a 'profound intellectual achievement' that the human brain was not designed to perform. [32]Unlike language, which is an innate capacity — infants begin acquiring it without formal instruction — reading must be taught, and the brain regions involved are repurposed from functions originally evolved for other tasks. Learning to read is, in this sense, a radical transformation of the brain.[/32]

The question of what reading does to us beyond the neurological level has occupied literary scholars, psychologists, and philosophers for decades. [33]Research consistently suggests that habitual fiction readers show greater capacity for empathy — the ability to understand and share the feelings of others — than non-readers. The mechanism proposed is that fiction requires us to inhabit the perspectives of characters quite different from ourselves, exercising the same mental capacities involved in real social understanding.[/33]

There is debate, however, about the direction of this relationship. [34]Some researchers argue that people who are already more empathetic are naturally more drawn to fiction, and that reading is a consequence of an empathetic temperament rather than its cause. Disentangling these two possibilities requires longitudinal studies that follow individuals over extended periods, and the results so far remain inconclusive.[/34]

The experience of deep or 'slow' reading — the kind of sustained, immersive attention that literary fiction typically demands — is increasingly regarded as something that requires active cultivation. [35]In an information environment characterised by brief texts, rapid scrolling, and constant interruption, the ability to read at length and in depth is, for many people, a skill that must be actively maintained through practice rather than one that sustains itself automatically.[/35]

[36]Despite predictions of its decline, literary reading persists. Sales of physical books have, in recent years, defied expectations, and reading groups, literary festivals, and public libraries continue to attract communities of readers. Whatever else the digital age has changed, it has not diminished the appetite for the experience of being lost inside a book.[/36]

questions.question: What does the opening paragraph identify as distinctive about reading compared to other art forms?
options: A) Reading provides a more realistic experience of other lives than film or theatre can offer.
B) Reading is more intellectually demanding than watching or listening to other art forms.
C) Reading is the only art form that allows people to experience other historical time periods.
D) Reading requires the reader's imagination to actively construct the world being described.
questions.explanation: Unlike film or theatre, reading 'requires the active participation of the reader's imagination in the construction of the world the text describes.'

questions.question: What does Maryanne Wolf's description of reading as a 'profound intellectual achievement' refer to?
options: A) The fact that reading requires the brain to redirect capacities developed for different purposes.
B) The cognitive effort required to understand complex or demanding literary texts.
C) The superior intelligence demonstrated by children who learn to read at an early age.
D) The academic advantages that extensive reading gives to children in school.
questions.explanation: Reading 'must be taught' and 'brain regions involved are repurposed from functions originally evolved for other tasks' — making it an achievement for an unprepared brain.

questions.question: What do researchers propose to explain why regular fiction readers show greater empathy?
options: A) That empathetic writers naturally produce fiction that develops empathy in its readers.
B) That empathetic people are more likely to be raised in households with many books.
C) That reading fiction provides detailed factual knowledge about human psychology and behaviour.
D) That regularly imagining others' perspectives through fiction develops real-world empathy.
questions.explanation: Fiction 'requires us to inhabit the perspectives of characters quite different from ourselves, exercising the same mental capacities involved in real social understanding.'

questions.question: What is the unresolved question about the relationship between reading and empathy?
options: A) Whether empathy developed through fiction transfers meaningfully to real social situations.
B) Whether empathy can be measured accurately enough to compare readers and non-readers.
C) Whether reading makes people more empathetic or whether empathetic people are drawn to reading.
D) Whether the empathetic benefits of reading extend to non-fiction as well as fiction.
questions.explanation: The debate is whether reading causes empathy or whether 'people who are already more empathetic are naturally more drawn to fiction' — an unresolved chicken-and-egg question.

questions.question: What challenge does the article identify for deep or 'slow' reading in the modern age?
options: A) It requires access to literary texts that are becoming increasingly difficult to obtain.
B) It needs to be consciously practised in an environment that constantly works against it.
C) It is incompatible with the kind of reading that digital devices make possible.
D) It is a skill that can only be properly developed through formal literary education.
questions.explanation: In a world of 'brief texts, rapid scrolling, and constant interruption,' deep reading is 'a skill that must be actively maintained through practice rather than one that sustains itself.'

questions.question: What does the final paragraph suggest about the future of literary reading?
options: A) That it will eventually be replaced by digital forms of storytelling and interactive entertainment.
B) That governments need to invest significantly more in public libraries to preserve reading culture.
C) That in spite of wide-ranging digital change, the desire to read deeply has not disappeared.
D) That it is declining more slowly than predicted but will ultimately disappear.
questions.explanation: Book sales have 'defied expectations' and communities of readers persist, showing the digital age 'has not diminished the appetite for the experience of being lost inside a book.'
